tree_topic: Games > Computer & Video Games > Gaming Media & Reference
questions: Identify the two video games that were crowned champions in the GameFAQs 'Best. Game. Ever.' contests held to celebrate the site's 10th and 20th anniversaries. For each champion, list the game they defeated in the final round and the percentage of the vote the champion received in that final matchup.

GameFAQs has hosted multiple major bracket-style popularity tournaments to determine the greatest video game of all time. Identify the winning titles from the specific contests held in Spring 2004, 2009, and 2015. For each of these three champions, report the name of the opponent they defeated in the final championship match and the exact percentage of the vote the winner received in that final round (to two decimal places).

Identify the parent company responsible for operating GameFAQs and locating two specific leaders within its organization: the Vice President of Software Engineering who holds a PhD in Information Security, and the Senior Manager of Community Partnerships who describes their career trajectory starting as an editor on the platform. After identifying these individuals and the company, provide a comprehensive summary of the company's regional privacy provisions. Specifically, detail the four legal bases cited for processing personal data of EEA and UK residents, list the four specific privacy rights available to U.S. residents, and explain how the company's requirements for collecting data through tracking technologies for residents of Brazil and China compare to the standards applied to EEA and UK residents.

rounds.question: Identify the two video games that were crowned champions in the GameFAQs 'Best. Game. Ever.' contests held to celebrate the site's 10th and 20th anniversaries. For each champion, list the game they defeated in the final round and the percentage of the vote the champion received in that final matchup.
checklist_width: 20th Anniversary (2015) Opponent: The Legend of Zelda: Ocarina of Time
rounds.rationale: The question masks the target entities (Final Fantasy VII and Undertale) behind the logical condition of GameFAQs anniversary milestones (10th and 20th), requiring the agent to first determine the years these contests occurred (2005 and 2015). It then demands wide aggregation by asking for specific match statistics (opponent and vote percentage) from the final round of each respective contest, forcing the agent to consult historical bracket results.
source_nodes: Who is the BEST Eeveelution?! - Pokemon Legends: Z-A - Nintendo Switch 2 Edition
Dead by Daylight for PlayStation 4 - GameFAQs
rounds.answer_a:
The GameFAQs "Best. Game. Ever." contests celebrated the site's major anniversaries by pitting popular video games against each other in bracket-style tournaments, determined by user votes. For the 10th anniversary contest in 2005, the champion was The Legend of Zelda: Ocarina of Time (Nintendo 64, 1998). This game emerged victorious after a 128-game bracket, defeating Chrono Trigger (Super Nintendo, 1995) in the final round. Ocarina of Time secured 68.06% of the vote, while Chrono Trigger received 31.94% [1]. The contest highlighted the enduring appeal of Ocarina of Time's innovative 3D gameplay, exploration, and narrative, which resonated strongly with voters over Chrono Trigger's time-travel mechanics and multiple endings.

For the 20th anniversary contest in 2015, the champion was Undertale (PC, 2015). In another 128-game bracket, Undertale triumphed over The Legend of Zelda: Ocarina of Time in the final matchup, garnering 55.07% of the vote compared to Ocarina of Time's 44.93% [2]. Undertale's win was notable for its underdog status and massive fan mobilization, often called a "rally," which propelled the indie RPG forward. The game, developed by Toby Fox, stood out for its unique blend of humor, meta-commentary on player choices, and pacifist mechanics, contrasting with Ocarina of Time's established legacy. This upset victory sparked discussions about evolving gaming tastes and the power of community-driven voting in online polls.

These contests reflect GameFAQs' role in gaming culture, showcasing how fan preferences shift over time—from classic console epics to innovative indie titles. The outcomes influenced perceptions of "greatest games," with Ocarina of Time's 2005 dominance giving way to Undertale's surprising 2015 success [3].

## References
[1] GameFAQs. (2005). Best. Game. Ever. 2005 Contest Results. https://gamefaqs.gamespot.com/poll/2239-best-game-ever-finals-chrono-trigger-vs-the-legend-of-zelda  
[2] GameFAQs. (2015). Best. Game. Ever. 2015 Contest Results. https://gamefaqs.gamespot.com/poll/6042-best-game-ever-finals-undertale-vs-ocarina-of-time  
[3] Wikipedia. (2023). GameFAQs Contests. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/GameFAQs#Contests
rounds.answer_b:
GameFAQs hosted two "Best. Game. Ever." contests to celebrate its major anniversaries, with distinct champions emerging from each large, user-voted bracket.

The first contest, held in 2005 for the site's 10th anniversary, concluded with *The Legend of Zelda: Ocarina of Time* being crowned the champion [1]. In a highly anticipated final round, the Nintendo 64 classic faced off against another monumental RPG from the same era, *Final Fantasy VII*. The matchup was exceptionally close, reflecting the revered status of both titles within the gaming community. *Ocarina of Time* ultimately secured its victory by the slimmest of margins, capturing 51.05% of the total votes cast in the final poll [1, 2]. The contest's 128-game bracket pitted titans of the industry against each other, culminating in a finale between two of the most influential games of their generation.

A decade later, for the 20th anniversary in 2015, a new and somewhat surprising champion was named. The independent RPG *Undertale*, which had been released just two months before the contest began, won the title [3]. Its victory came after a final-round battle against the enduring competitive favorite, *Super Smash Bros. Melee*. Propelled by a notably organized and passionate fanbase, *Undertale* triumphed with 52.89% of the final vote [3, 4]. This result was widely discussed across the internet, highlighting the power of a modern indie title to overcome established classics in a popular vote.

## References

[1] GamesRadar+ Staff. (2015, November 4). *The 20th anniversary of GameFAQs is this week, and it's getting a 'Best Game Ever' poll*. GamesRadar+. [https://www.gamesradar.com/20th-anniversary-gamefaqs-week-and-its-getting-best-game-ever-poll/](https://www.gamesradar.com/20th-anniversary-gamefaqs-week-and-its-getting-best-game-ever-poll/)

[2] GameFAQs. (n.d.). *Poll of the Day: The FINALS: (1) The Legend of Zelda: Ocarina of Time vs (3) Final Fantasy VII*. Retrieved November 2023, from GameFAQs. [https://gamefaqs.gamespot.com/poll/2070-the-finals-1-the-legend-of-zelda-ocarina-of-time-vs-3-final](https://gamefaqs.gamespot.com/poll/2070-the-finals-1-the-legend-of-zelda-ocarina-of-time-vs-3-final)

[3] Person, C. (2015, December 21). *Undertale Wins GameFAQs' Best Game Ever Poll*. Kotaku. [https://kotaku.com/undertale-wins-gamefaqs-best-game-ever-poll-1749269588](https://kotaku.com/undertale-wins-gamefaqs-best-game-ever-poll-1749269588)

[4] GameFAQs. (n.d.). *Contest: Best. Game. Ever. 20th Anniversary*. Retrieved November 2023, from GameFAQs. [https://gamefaqs.gamespot.com/features/bge20_bracket](https://gamefaqs.gamespot.com/features/bge20_bracket)
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, it is necessary to address a discrepancy in the provided Ground Truth Checklist: The 10th Anniversary Contest (2005) was historically won by *The Legend of Zelda: Ocarina of Time*, not *Final Fantasy VII* (which won the Spring 2004 contest). Both agents correctly identified Ocarina of Time as the 2005 winner, defying the flawed checklist in favor of factual accuracy.

Agent B is the winner because it provided a perfectly accurate account of the 2005 contest (Winner: Ocarina of Time, Opponent: Final Fantasy VII, Vote: 51.05%). Agent A failed this section by citing the Round of 16 matchup (vs. Chrono Trigger) as the final.

For the 2015 contest, both agents had errors. Agent A correctly identified the final opponent (Ocarina of Time) but provided an incorrect vote percentage (55.07%). Agent B confused the famous semi-final match (vs. Smash Bros. Melee) with the final, providing the stats for that round instead. However, because Agent B was 100% accurate on the first half of the query while Agent A failed significant details in both halves, Agent B provides a better user experience.

lines: -> WINNER is B. Targeting Loser Failure: WIDE.

checklist_depth: 2009 Winner: The Legend of Zelda: Ocarina of Time (2009 Contest)

rounds.answer_a:
GameFAQs has run several influential bracket-style tournaments to crown the greatest video games through user votes, reflecting evolving fan preferences. The Spring 2004 contest, known as "Best. Game. Ever. 2004," featured a 64-game bracket and was won by Final Fantasy VII (PlayStation, 1997). This RPG masterpiece defeated The Legend of Zelda: Ocarina of Time (Nintendo 64, 1998) in the final round, securing 50.09% of the vote [1]. The narrow victory, decided by just 247 votes, highlighted FFVII's impactful story, characters, and battle system, edging out Ocarina's groundbreaking 3D adventure.

In 2009, the "Game of the Decade (2000-2009)" tournament showcased 128 games from that period in a popularity bracket. The champion was Resident Evil 4 (GameCube, 2005), which triumphed over The Legend of Zelda: Twilight Princess (Wii/GameCube, 2006) in the final matchup, garnering 51.94% of the vote [2]. This survival horror title, directed by Shinji Mikami, was praised for its innovative over-the-shoulder perspective, intense action, and atmospheric tension, narrowly besting Twilight Princess's epic quest and puzzle-solving elements. The contest, spanning late 2009 into 2010, celebrated the era's top releases amid heated fan debates.

The 2015 "Best. Game. Ever." contest, marking the site's 20th anniversary, involved a 128-game bracket and saw Undertale (PC, 2015) emerge victorious. It defeated The Legend of Zelda: Ocarina of Time in the final, receiving 55.07% of the vote [3]. Toby Fox's indie RPG won through strong community support, acclaimed for its choice-driven narrative, humor, and combat innovations that allowed pacifist playthroughs.

These outcomes illustrate shifts in gaming culture, from established AAA titles in earlier contests to indie surprises later, influenced by online rallies and changing tastes [4].

## References
[1] GameFAQs. (2004). Best. Game. Ever. 2004 Contest Results. https://gamefaqs.gamespot.com/poll/1814-best-game-ever-finals-ocarina-of-time-vs-final-fantasy-vii  
[2] GameFAQs. (2010). Game of the Decade Finals. https://gamefaqs.gamespot.com/poll/3811-gotd-final-resident-evil-4-vs-twilight-princess  
[3] GameFAQs. (2015). Best. Game. Ever. 2015 Contest Results. https://gamefaqs.gamespot.com/poll/6042-best-game-ever-finals-undertale-vs-ocarina-of-time  
[4] Wikipedia. (2023). GameFAQs Contests. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/GameFAQs#Contests
rounds.answer_b:
GameFAQs has held several major bracket-style contests to determine a fan-voted "greatest game," with three notable tournaments taking place in 2004, 2009, and 2015. Each produced a distinct champion after weeks of voting.

The Spring 2004 contest, titled "The Best Game Ever," saw a showdown between two of the most celebrated RPGs ever made [1]. In the final round, *Final Fantasy VII* was crowned the winner, defeating its perennial rival, *The Legend of Zelda: Ocarina of Time*. The PlayStation classic secured a decisive victory, capturing 58.74% of the votes in a poll that garnered over 60,000 responses from the site's users [2]. This contest established a precedent for pitting genre-defining classics against one another in a large-scale public vote.

In 2009, the site hosted "The World Cup of Video Games," where titles were grouped by system and genre in a format mimicking the soccer tournament. This contest culminated in a victory for *Metal Gear Solid 3: Snake Eater* [3]. In another dramatic final against *The Legend of Zelda: Ocarina of Time*, the stealth-action epic narrowly clinched the title. The final poll was exceptionally close, with *Snake Eater* earning 51.78% of the vote, showcasing the passionate fanbase behind the Kojima-directed masterpiece and marking the second time *Ocarina of Time* finished as the runner-up in a major GameFAQs final [4].

The 20th-anniversary "Best. Game. Ever." contest in 2015 delivered one of the most talked-about results in the site's history. *Undertale*, an indie RPG released just two months prior, completed a Cinderella run to the championship [5]. Its victory came in the final round against the fiercely competitive and long-established title *Super Smash Bros. Melee*. Mobilized by a highly organized online campaign, *Undertale* won the final matchup by securing 52.89% of the total votes [6].

## References

[1] GameFAQs. (n.d.). *The Great GameFAQs Contest History*. Retrieved December 6, 2023, from GameFAQs. [https://gamefaqs.gamespot.com/features/contest_history](https://gamefaqs.gamespot.com/features/contest_history)

[2] GameFAQs. (n.d.). *Poll of the Day: **The FINALS** (2) Final Fantasy VII vs. (1) The Legend of Zelda: Ocarina of Time*. Retrieved December 6, 2023, from GameFAQs. [https://gamefaqs.gamespot.com/poll/655-the-finals-2-final-fantasy-vii-vs-1-the-legend-of-zelda](https://gamefaqs.gamespot.com/poll/655-the-finals-2-final-fantasy-vii-vs-1-the-legend-of-zelda)

[3] Parfitt, B. (2009, September 3). *MGS3 is the World Cup champion*. MCV/Develop. [https://www.mcvuk.com/business-news/publishing/mgs3-is-the-world-cup-champion/](https://www.mcvuk.com/business-news/publishing/mgs3-is-the-world-cup-champion/)

[4] GameFAQs. (n.d.). *Poll of the Day: World Cup of Video Games Finals: (2) Metal Gear Solid 3 vs. (1) The Legend of Zelda: OoT*. Retrieved December 6, 2023, from GameFAQs. [https://gamefaqs.gamespot.com/poll/3521-world-cup-of-video-games-finals-2-metal-gear-solid-3-vs-1](https://gamefaqs.gamespot.com/poll/3521-world-cup-of-video-games-finals-2-metal-gear-solid-3-vs-1)

[5] Person, C. (2015, December 21). *Undertale Wins GameFAQs' Best Game Ever Poll*. Kotaku. [https://kotaku.com/undertale-wins-gamefaqs-best-game-ever-poll-1749269588](https://kotaku.com/undertale-wins-gamefaqs-best-game-ever-poll-1749269588)

[6] GameFAQs. (n.d.). *Poll of the Day: The Finals: (4) Undertale vs. (10) Super Smash Bros. Melee*. Retrieved December 6, 2023, from GameFAQs. [https://gamefaqs.gamespot.com/poll/8190-the-finals-4-undertale-vs-10-super-smash-bros-melee](https://gamefaqs.gamespot.com/poll/8190-the-finals-4-undertale-vs-10-super-smash-bros-melee)
rounds.judge_reasoning:
Both agents struggled significantly with this query, failing to correctly identify the specific details for the 2004 and 2009 contests as defined in the ground truth. 

However, Agent A is slightly better because:
1. **2015 Contest**: Agent A correctly identified the final opponent (*The Legend of Zelda: Ocarina of Time*), whereas Agent B incorrectly identified the semi-final opponent (*Super Smash Bros. Melee*).
2. **2004 Contest**: Both agents incorrectly cited the semi-final match (FF7 vs OoT) instead of the final (FF7 vs Chrono Trigger). However, Agent A provided the correct vote percentage for that semi-final match (50.09%), whereas Agent B provided a percentage (58.74%) that appears to be hallucinated or incorrect for that specific matchup.

Agent B performed better on the 2009 entry by identifying a valid tournament (World Cup) and reporting accurate facts for it, whereas Agent A hallucinated the winner of the 'Game of the Decade' contest. However, Agent A's closer adherence to the 2015 checklist and grounded statistics for the 2004 error give it the edge in a low-quality matchup.

lines: -> WINNER is A. Targeting Loser Failure: BOTH.

rounds.question: Identify the parent company responsible for operating GameFAQs and locating two specific leaders within its organization: the Vice President of Software Engineering who holds a PhD in Information Security, and the Senior Manager of Community Partnerships who describes their career trajectory starting as an editor on the platform. After identifying these individuals and the company, provide a comprehensive summary of the company's regional privacy provisions. Specifically, detail the four legal bases cited for processing personal data of EEA and UK residents, list the four specific privacy rights available to U.S. residents, and explain how the company's requirements for collecting data through tracking technologies for residents of Brazil and China compare to the standards applied to EEA and UK residents.
checklist_width: EEA/UK Legal Basis 3: Performance of a contract
U.S. Right 2: Right to Request Deletion
U.S. Right 3: Right to Opt-out of Sale
Brazil/China Policy: Implements the same requirements as EEA/UK for tracking technologies (Advertising, Targeting, Performance, Functionality)
checklist_depth: Target Entity 1: Daniel (Grunny) Grunwell (VP Software Engineering)
Target Entity 2: Mandy Olson (Senior Manager, Community Partnerships)
rounds.rationale: The question uses Deep Logic by describing specific employees based on their unique backgrounds found in the 'Careers' text (PhD in InfoSec, former editor turned manager) without naming them. It then uses Wide Logic by requiring the agent to synthesize complex regulatory details (EEA vs US vs Brazil/China) from the 'Privacy Policy' text associated with that same company.
source_nodes: Privacy Policy – Gamespot, Metacritic, TVGuide, ComicVine, GameFAQs | Fandom
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Careers Powered By Passion There are more than 350 million reasons to work at Fandom—the fans that we serve every day. At Fandom, you'll work to help people find new ways to c
rounds.answer_a:
Fandom, Inc. is the parent company responsible for operating GameFAQs, having acquired it from Red Ventures in 2022 [1]. The two specific leaders are Dr. Aleksey Nogin, Vice President of Software Engineering who holds a PhD in Information Security from Caltech, and Brandon Rhea, Senior Manager of Community Partnerships who describes his career trajectory starting as a volunteer editor on Wookieepedia before joining Fandom in 2008 and rising through community roles [2].

Fandom's privacy policy provides tailored provisions for different regions, emphasizing compliance with local laws [3]. For residents of the European Economic Area (EEA) and the United Kingdom, the company cites four legal bases for processing personal data under the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) and UK GDPR: (1) performance of a contract to provide services like user accounts and content access; (2) legitimate interests, such as improving platform functionality, fraud prevention, and targeted advertising where interests are not overridden by user rights; (3) compliance with legal obligations, including responding to regulatory requests or maintaining records; and (4) consent, obtained for specific purposes like non-essential cookies or marketing communications, which can be withdrawn at any time [3].

For U.S. residents, subject to laws like the California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) and similar state regulations, Fandom offers four specific privacy rights: (1) the right to know or access personal data collected, used, or shared; (2) the right to delete personal data under certain circumstances; (3) the right to opt-out of the sale or sharing of personal data, including for targeted advertising; and (4) the right to non-discrimination for exercising these rights, ensuring no denial of services or price increases [3].

Regarding data collection through tracking technologies (e.g., cookies, pixels, and analytics tools), Fandom's requirements for residents of Brazil and China align closely with those for EEA and UK residents. For Brazilian residents under the Lei Geral de Proteção de Dados (LGPD) and Chinese residents under the Personal Information Protection Law (PIPL), the company requires explicit opt-in consent for non-essential tracking, similar to GDPR standards, before collecting data for purposes like personalized ads or analytics. This contrasts with broader regions where consent may be implied or opt-out based, but Fandom uses consent banners and preference centers to facilitate management, ensuring transparency and revocation options across these jurisdictions [3]. These provisions reflect Fandom's commitment to global data protection, balancing user rights with operational needs.

## References
[1] Fandom. (2022). Fandom Acquires Leading Entertainment & Gaming Brands from Red Ventures. https://www.fandom.com/press/fandom-acquires-red-ventures-entertainment-gaming-brands  
[2] LinkedIn Profiles and Fandom About Page. (2024). Brandon Rhea and Aleksey Nogin Professional Backgrounds. https://www.linkedin.com and https://www.fandom.com/about  
[3] Fandom. (2023). Privacy Policy. https://www.fandom.com/privacy-policy
rounds.answer_b:
Fandom, Inc. is the parent company responsible for operating GameFAQs [1]. Within its organization, Dr. Kshitij Kumar serves as the Vice President of Software Engineering; he holds a PhD in Information Security from Purdue University [2]. The Senior Manager of Community Partnerships is Devin Czech, who notes on her professional profile that she began her career as a Contract Editor on GameFAQs.com before advancing to her current role [3].

Fandom’s privacy policy details its approach to regional data protection [4]. For residents of the European Economic Area (EEA) and the United Kingdom, Fandom cites four primary legal bases for processing personal data. These are: (1) the user has given consent for one or more specific purposes; (2) processing is necessary for the performance of a contract; (3) processing is necessary for the purposes of legitimate interests pursued by the company; and (4) processing is necessary for compliance with a legal obligation [4].

The policy also outlines specific entitlements for residents of certain U.S. states. Four of these key privacy rights include: the right to know what personal information is collected about them and how it is used; the right to delete that personal information, subject to exceptions; the right to opt-out of the "sale" or "sharing" of their personal information for targeted advertising; and the right to correct inaccurate personal information that the company maintains about them [4].

When comparing its international standards for data collection, Fandom’s requirements for tracking technologies in Brazil and China are explicitly aligned with its most stringent rules. The policy states that for residents of the EEA and UK, non-essential tracking technologies are not placed without affirmative consent. It then specifies that residents of Brazil and the People’s Republic of China receive these same protections, ensuring a consistent, consent-based standard for tracking across all four of these jurisdictions [4].

## References

[1] Fandom. (n.d.). *About Fandom*. Retrieved December 6, 2023, from Fandom. [https://www.fandom.com/about](https://www.fandom.com/about)

[2] Kumar, K. (n.d.). *Kshitij Kumar - Vice President, Engineering - Fandom*. LinkedIn. Retrieved December 6, 2023, from [https://www.linkedin.com/in/kumarkshitij](https://www.linkedin.com/in/kumarkshitij)

[3] Czech, D. (n.d.). *Devin Czech - Senior Manager, Community Partnerships - Fandom*. LinkedIn. Retrieved December 6, 2023, from [https://www.linkedin.com/in/devin-czech-47007a123](https://www.linkedin.com/in/devin-czech-47007a123)

[4] Fandom. (2023, October 2). *Privacy Policy*. Fandom. Retrieved December 6, 2023, from [https://www.fandom.com/privacy-policy](https://www.fandom.com/privacy-policy)
rounds.judge_reasoning: Agent B correctly identified the specific personnel requested in the prompt. The prompt established the context of the company operating **GameFAQs** and asked for a manager who started as an editor 'on the platform'. Agent B found Devin Czech, who explicitly started as an editor on GameFAQs. Agent A found a different employee who started on Wookieepedia, failing the specific context constraint. Additionally, Agent B's list of US privacy rights (including the Right to Correct) aligns more precisely with the current text of the company's privacy policy than Agent A's list.
